result.reasoning: Issue #113: GateReview was a frozen dataclass with a mutable-in-place, unhashable Mapping payload (an escape of the codebase's own documented issue #7). The fix adds GateReview.__post_init__ (src/assay_engine/pipeline.py:90-92) which calls object.__setattr__('payload', freeze_mapping(self.payload)). This is the SAME pattern every other frozen mapping-bearing record uses (verified ProvenanceEntry in provenance.py:65-66 calls freeze_mapping identically), so it is the canonical root-cause fix, not a hack or over-claim. I confirmed at runtime that the fix deep-freezes: payload becomes a FrozenDict, nested mappings become FrozenDict, lists become tuples, and the record is hashable. The regression test test_gate_review_payload_is_frozen_and_hashable (tests/test_pipeline.py:451-466) asserts all three halves of the finding: isinstance FrozenDict, mutation raises, hash(r) succeeds. It passes on the current branch. Critically, I proved it has real regression-catching power by reconstructing the pre-fix dataclass (no __post_init__): payload was NOT a FrozenDict, in-place mutation succeeded (bug present), and hash() raised TypeError — i.e. the test would fail on every assertion without the fix. Both appropriate and complete are satisfied.
result.evidence: src/assay_engine/pipeline.py:80-92 (GateReview frozen dataclass + __post_init__ freeze_mapping); src/assay_engine/_frozen.py:61-107 (freeze/freeze_mapping deep-immutable conversion); src/assay_engine/provenance.py:65-66 (ProvenanceEntry uses identical pattern — consistency); tests/test_pipeline.py:451-466 (regression test, passes). Pre-fix simulation: isinstance FrozenDict=False, mutation succeeded, hash raised TypeError — test catches the regression. Post-fix runtime: payload type=FrozenDict, nested type=FrozenDict, ids=tuple, hash ok. pytest: 1 passed.

result.reasoning:
Issue #113: GateReview was a frozen dataclass with a `payload: Mapping[str, Any]` field but, unlike every other frozen mapping-bearing record in the engine, lacked a __post_init__ that normalized the payload through freeze_mapping — reproducing both halves of the codebase's documented issue #7 (mutable-in-place dict + unhashable record).

CURRENT STATE on harden/pass-1 is the soundly-fixed version:
- src/assay_engine/pipeline.py:90-92 adds `def __post_init__` that calls `object.__setattr__(self, "payload", freeze_mapping(self.payload))`. This is the identical, idiomatic pattern used across the engine (the same approach ProvenanceEntry/GateDecision/etc. use), so it addresses the root cause directly with no hack and no over-claim. freeze_mapping returns a FrozenDict whose leaves are deep-frozen and which has a real __hash__, fixing both the mutability and hashability halves.

APPROPRIATE: yes. The fix is the canonical engine pattern, not a workaround.

COMPLETE: yes, with a discriminating regression test. test_gate_review_payload_is_frozen_and_hashable (tests/test_pipeline.py:451) constructs a GateReview with a nested dict + list payload and asserts (1) isinstance(payload, FrozenDict), (2) mutation `r.payload["a"]=2` raises, (3) hash(r) succeeds. I ran it against the venv: PASSES. I then verified the test actually catches a regression by reproducing the pre-fix state (frozen dataclass with a raw dict payload): isinstance(FrozenDict) is False (assert fails), mutation succeeds (pytest.raises fails), and hash(r) raises TypeError. All three assertions flip without the fix, so the test is not a no-op — it would genuinely catch a regression.
result.evidence: src/assay_engine/pipeline.py:80-92 (GateReview frozen dataclass + __post_init__ calling freeze_mapping); import at pipeline.py:38. Regression test at tests/test_pipeline.py:451-466. freeze_mapping/FrozenDict at src/assay_engine/_frozen.py:24,50,105 (FrozenDict.__hash__ + deep freeze). Ran `.venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/test_pipeline.py::test_gate_review_payload_is_frozen_and_hashable -q` -> 1 passed. Pre-fix reproduction showed isinstance(FrozenDict)=False, mutation did NOT raise, hash raised TypeError — confirming the test discriminates.

result.reasoning: Issue #111: gate decisions on the durable LangGraph path were recorded only as plain dicts in graph state ('gate_decisions'), bypassing the hash-chained ProvenanceTrail that GOVERNANCE §3 calls the integrity spine. APPROPRIATE: the fix is a sound seam, not a hack. make_gate_node accepts an optional recorder= callback and, after apply_gate_decision validates the resumed operator decision, calls recorder(rec) for each recorded decision (gatenode.py lines 129-131), bridging durable-path decisions into the same ProvenanceTrail.record(...) hash chain used by the synchronous run_study path. Keeping recorder optional/lazy is correct given the constraints (lazy optional orchestration backend; engine must not force the extra) — and the docs say so plainly rather than over-claiming. The scoping docs are honest and accurate: graph.py docstring (lines 61-64) and docs/GOVERNANCE.md §3 (lines 105-110) both explicitly state the durable graph path records to LangGraph state, NOT the tamper-evident chained trail, unless wired with recorder=. COMPLETE: it fully resolves the documented (honestly-scoped, opt-in) fix and ships a genuine regression test. test_gate_node_bridges_decision_into_provenance_trail (tests/test_orchestration_graph.py lines 110-124) wires a real ProvenanceTrail recorder, drives the node, then verify_records(trail.entries) proves an intact hash chain and asserts a kind=='gate' entry carrying the decision. I confirmed the test is not a tautology: it passes with the fix, and after I temporarily removed the recorder-bridge loop from gatenode.py it FAILED with assert ([]) (no gate entry landed in the trail); I then restored the file (git diff --stat clean) and all 20 tests in the file pass. The test would therefore actually catch a regression.

result.reasoning:
Issue #102: run_json applied retry budgets multiplicatively (retry-storm, no overall deadline). I verified the CURRENT state on branch harden/pass-1.

ROOT-CAUSE FIX (appropriate=true): The fix soundly addresses the actual cause, not a hack.
1. run_json (seam.py:602-622) now calls `_run_with_retries(attempt_req, deadline=deadline)` OUTSIDE the try/except that re-rolls. Only `extract_json(text)` is wrapped in the `except ReasoningError` handler, so a transient/rate/permanent error PROPAGATES instead of triggering an outer re-roll. This eliminates the budget multiplication (the documented (MAX_RETRIES+1)^2 / 9x storm). Permanent errors already propagate via `_run_with_retries` (line 518).
2. An overall wall-clock deadline (RUN_JSON_DEADLINE, line 109/597) is computed once and (a) checked at the top of each re-roll iteration (line 603-604) and (b) threaded into `_run_with_retries` -> `_sleep_bounded` (lines 493-504, 523, 528) which caps every backoff sleep by the remaining budget and raises once the deadline passes. This bounds the rate-limit 450s-per-iteration blowup the finding described. The deadline default is configurable and floored at 1.0s; backoff min(secs, remaining) is correct.

COMPLETE (complete=true): The fix fully resolves the finding AND has genuine regression tests. Three named tests exist and all pass (10/10 in the #102-related selection; 61/61 full file). I confirmed each test ACTUALLY catches a regression (does not pass with/without the fix):
- test_run_json_does_not_reroll_on_reasoning_error_no_multiplication: asserts _attempt called MAX_RETRIES+1 (=3) times, not 3*6. I reverted to the old buggy behavior (catching ReasoningError around _run_with_retries) -> the test FAILED with `assert 18 == 3` (the 3x6 multiplication), confirming it catches the core bug.
- test_run_json_overall_deadline_bounds_rerolls: asserts only 1 attempt runs when the deadline is already passed (JSON_REROLLS=50). I removed the deadline guard -> test FAILED with `assert 51 == 1`.
- test_run_json_varies_seed_and_never_lowers_temperature: confirms re-roll only on parse failure with seed [0,1,2] and monotonic non-decreasing temp <=1.0; passes.
Source was restored clean after each PoC (git diff --quiet -> CLEAN).
result.evidence: src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py:493-504 (_sleep_bounded deadline bound), :507-529 (_run_with_retries deadline param), :578-622 (run_json: deadline computed line 597, per-iter check 603-604, _run_with_retries called outside re-roll try at 617, only extract_json in except at 618-621). Tests: tests/test_reasoning.py:358-374, :377-391, :394-411. PoC results: reverting fix -> test_run_json_does_not_reroll_on_reasoning_error_no_multiplication failed `assert 18 == (2+1)`; removing deadline guard -> test_run_json_overall_deadline_bounds_rerolls failed `assert 51 == 1`. Full suite: 61 passed in 0.26s. Source restored clean (git diff --quiet: CLEAN).

result.reasoning: Issue #114: ProvenanceTrail.record performed a non-atomic check-then-act (read seq/prev_hash, then clock+digest+freeze, then append) with no lock. APPROPRIATE=true: the current fix on harden/pass-1 is sound and root-cause-directed. provenance.py:114 adds self._lock = threading.Lock(); record() (line 120) wraps the whole read-compute-append in `with self._lock:` by delegating to _record_locked (lines 123-160). No over-claim; the inline comment and docstring honestly describe it. I proved the mechanism: with a clock that yields (sleeps 0.2ms) to widen the read->append window, the UNLOCKED trail corrupts the chain (verify() raises 'provenance entry 1 has seq 0 (reordered or removed)' — two entries claimed the same seq) while the LOCKED trail verifies cleanly with 400 contiguous seqs. So the lock genuinely fixes the race. COMPLETE=false: the named regression test tests/test_provenance.py:170 test_record_is_thread_safe_under_concurrency (8 threads x 50 records, Barrier for contention, asserts len==400 + verify() + contiguous seqs) does NOT actually catch the regression on the real runtime. I ran the exact committed test body with the lock removed (NullLock) 30 times on the venv (Python 3.14.5, GIL enabled per sys._is_gil_enabled()==True): it caught the corruption 0/30 times — it passes equally with and without the fix. Under CPython's GIL the pure-Python read-compute-append window for this workload is too narrow to interleave; the race only manifests when a yield point (e.g. the injected clock taking a moment, which the real code's clock()+digest do in production but the test's default _utc_now does not) widens the window. By the task's own criterion ('a test that passes equally with/without the fix does NOT count'), this regression test is non-discriminating as written. To make it genuine it should inject a clock (or monkeypatch a yield) that opens the read->append window, e.g. a clock that time.sleep(0.0002)s, which reliably exposes the unlocked race (CORRUPTED) vs locked (OK).
result.evidence: FIX (sound): /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/provenance.py:114 `self._lock = threading.Lock()`; line 120-121 `with self._lock: return self._record_locked(...)`; read-compute-append now atomic in _record_locked (lines 123-160: seq=len(self._entries) @126, prev_hash @127, clock/digest @129-144, append @159). TEST: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/tests/test_provenance.py:170-189. POC RESULTS — slow-clock discriminator: UNLOCKED -> 'CORRUPTED: provenance entry 1 has seq 0 (reordered or removed)'; LOCKED -> 'OK'. COMMITTED-TEST non-discrimination: committed test body with lock removed (default clock) on Python 3.14.5 GIL-enabled caught regression in 0/30 runs. Committed test currently passes: `pytest tests/test_provenance.py::test_record_is_thread_safe_under_concurrency` -> 1 passed.

result.reasoning:
Issue #117: QdrantVectorStore owned and never closed a self-constructed QdrantClient (httpx pool / gRPC channel leak). The current state on harden/pass-1 soundly addresses the root cause: __init__ records ownership via _owns_client = client is None (line 66); a new close() (lines 69-74) closes the underlying client ONLY when owned, guarded by getattr/callable so it is safe even if the client lacks close(); and __enter__/__exit__ (lines 76-80) add context-manager support so the resource is released deterministically. This is the correct, non-hacky fix and it does not over-claim — it deliberately leaves an injected client to the caller, which is the right ownership contract.

Complete: both halves are covered by targeted regression tests. test_vector_store_closes_owned_client (lines 296-312) injects NO client (so the store owns it), monkeypatches get_qdrant_client to a fake, uses the store as a context manager, and asserts created.closed is True — this would FAIL without the close()/__exit__ fix (the fake would never be closed), so it genuinely catches a regression. test_vector_store_upsert_streams_batches_and_owns_client asserts the complementary invariant (injected client fc.closed is False after store.close(), line 293), which would FAIL if close() ignored ownership and closed an injected client. The fix correctly resolves the named LOW finding with regression coverage that actually discriminates fixed vs. unfixed behavior.

Tests: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/tests/test_persistence.py lines 269-293 (injected client NOT closed) and 296-312 (owned client IS closed on context exit). Ran in venv: qdrant_client installed; `.venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/test_persistence.py::test_vector_store_closes_owned_client tests/test_persistence.py::test_vector_store_upsert_streams_batches_and_owns_client -q` => 2 passed. Counterfactual: removing close()/__exit__ would leave created.closed False, failing line 312 — test discriminates fix from no-fix.

result.reasoning:
Issue #114: ProvenanceTrail.record() non-atomic check-then-act (not thread-safe).

APPROPRIATE = true. The fix (src/assay_engine/provenance.py) adds self._lock = threading.Lock() in __init__ and makes record() acquire it before delegating to a new _record_locked(), wrapping the entire read-compute-append (seq=len(self._entries), prev_hash read -> clock/digest/freeze -> self._entries.append) in one critical section. This directly and soundly fixes the root cause with no hack and no over-claim (docstring accurately says 'Thread-safe'). I verified the lock genuinely prevents corruption: with a realistic GIL-yield point in the compute window, the unlocked (pre-fix) path produces wrong count + non-contiguous seqs + verify() failure (False,False), while the locked path stays intact (True,True).

COMPLETE = false. The named regression test test_record_is_thread_safe_under_concurrency (tests/test_provenance.py:170) does NOT actually catch the regression. It passes WITH the fix (pytest 15/15) and ALSO passes WITHOUT the fix: routing the exact same 8-threads x 50-records barrier workload through the unlocked _record_locked path passed 50/50 runs. Under CPython's GIL (sys._is_gil_enabled()==True, Python 3.14.5 in the venv) the read-compute-append window has no reliable GIL-yielding bytecode for the tiny test payloads (hashlib.sha256 doesn't release the GIL for small inputs; datetime.now is fast and the test does not tune sys.setswitchinterval), so the race never manifests with the default clock. Per the task's explicit criterion ('a test that passes equally with/without the fix does NOT count'), this test is not a valid guard. A correct guard must force interleaving (e.g. a yielding/slow injected clock or setswitchinterval tuning), which I showed reliably distinguishes locked from unlocked.

The in-scope optimization (callers that directly invoke build_baseline_artifact can now skip recompute) is fully realized. result.reasoning:
Issue #119 (LOW perf): corpus_fingerprint recomputed redundantly. CONFIRMER #1 verdict: appropriate=true, complete=true (judged against the honestly-deferred scope).

APPROPRIATE: The fix soundly addresses the root cause at the function API level. build_baseline_artifact (determinism.py:88-125) now accepts a keyword-only corpus_hash param; line 108 reuses it when provided (`corpus_hash if corpus_hash is not None else corpus_fingerprint(corpus)`), eliminating the redundant full O(corpus-bytes) canonical sort+JSON+hash. The passed hash flows correctly into both input_hashes['corpus'] (line 111) and the artifact's corpus_fingerprint field (line 124). No hack, no over-claim. The seed derivation still works because the corpus hash is among input_hashes.

DEFERRAL IS HONESTLY SCOPED: I verified the documented deferral is real, not an excuse. pipeline.py:355 calls plan.baseline_builder.build(corpus, claim_guard=guard) — the BaselineBuilder Protocol boundary. The builder internally produces its own fingerprint; pipeline.py:356 cross-checks baseline.corpus_fingerprint against the ingest-time cfp (line 299). Threading cfp into the builder so it could forward corpus_hash would indeed require a breaking change to build()'s signature — not justified for a LOW perf item. The fix_update note states exactly this. Judged against that honestly-scoped fix (improve the helper API, defer cross-Protocol e2e dedup), the finding is fully resolved.

CI confirms it — ci.yml runs bare `mypy` (lines 30, 67) with NO --python-version flag, across matrix python-version ["3.11","3.12","3.13","3.14"], so each lane now type-checks against its own version. The fix is sound, not a hack, no over-claim. result.reasoning: Issue #118: QdrantVectorStore.upsert previously materialized the entire corpus of PointStructs (each with a per-vector list(v) copy) before sending any batch, so chunking bounded only HTTP payload, not peak memory. The committed fix (src/assay_engine/persistence/vectorstore.py:100-108) builds each batch of PointStructs lazily inside the chunking loop via range(start, min(start+batch_size, len(ids))), so at most batch_size PointStructs are materialized at a time. This soundly addresses the root cause (peak memory now bounded by the batch) with no hack and an accurate code comment — APPROPRIATE. The named regression test test_vector_store_upsert_builds_one_batch_at_a_time (tests/test_persistence.py:315) is a real discriminating test: a TrackingVectors wrapper records each __getitem__ consumption and FakeClient.upsert snapshots how many vectors have been consumed at each send, asserting [2,4,6]. I empirically confirmed the test actually runs (qdrant_client is installed in the venv, not skipped), PASSES with the fix, and FAILS with [6,6,6] != [2,4,6] when I reverted the code to an all-up-front list comprehension — so it would catch a real regression. COMPLETE.

result.reasoning:
Finding #122 (CONFIRMER #1, index 9) is appropriate and complete on branch harden/pass-1.

ROOT-CAUSE FIX (sound, no hack/over-claim): src/assay_engine/pipeline.py now (1) calls bootstrap_tracing() at line 283 — wiring the global OTel provider to the local collector once per run, idempotent and best-effort; and (2) derives corr_id = run_id or uuid.uuid4().hex (line 284) and wraps ALL phase spans in run_trace_context(corr_id) via an ExitStack entered at line 287 before the first phase span (phase:INGEST at 290) and closed in the finally block at line 557, so baggage is detached even on failure. corr_id falls back to the experiment-tracker run_id when a tracker is present, achieving the cross-store correlation the finding required. run_trace_context (observability/tracing.py:146-167) attaches the 'assay.run_id' baggage and detaches the token on exit; it is a no-op when run_id is None or OpenTelemetry is absent, respecting the lazy-optional-backend / no-load-bearing-tracing constraint. Both root causes named in the finding (never bootstrapping, never stamping baggage) are fixed.

REGRESSION TEST IS REAL (would catch a regression): tests/test_pipeline.py::test_run_study_correlates_spans_to_run_id injects a CapturingTracer whose span() records baggage.get_baggage('assay.run_id') on every phase span, runs a full DISCOVERY study with a tracker returning 'run-xyz', then asserts tracer.seen == {'run-xyz'} (every phase span saw the run id, none None) AND that baggage is detached after the run (None). I ran it against the venv: PASS. NEGATIVE CONTROL: I commented out the trace_ctx.enter_context(run_trace_context(corr_id)) line and re-ran — the test FAILED (seen would be {None}), then restored the file and re-confirmed PASS. So the test is not vacuous; it discriminates fixed vs unfixed.

SCOPE NOTE (honest): the test verifies baggage is present in the active context during each span (in-process correlation + clean detach), not that baggage is materialized onto exported span attributes (which would require a baggage-span-processor at the collector). This matches the finding's stated concern (baggage stamping for correlation) and the lazy/optional collector design; integration test tests/integration/test_observability_live.py additionally exercises the attach/detach contract. No over-claim.
